metadata.summary: A system for capturing, storing, and retrieving proven solutions from development sessions so LLM agents apply consistent patterns across tasks and projects.

content.title: Pattern Memory
content.summary: A system for capturing, storing, and retrieving proven solutions from development sessions so LLM agents apply consistent patterns across tasks and projects. Patterns are stored in `.aidocs/memory/patterns.md` with a confidence score (0.5 initial → 0.9+ proven), graduated by number of successful uses. High-confidence patterns (0.8+) are synced to `CLAUDE.md` for immediate availability in new sessions. The rule: capture when a non-obvious solution works in 2+ distinct contexts; never capture obvious best practices or one-off fixes.

why.p: Each task session starts fresh. Without an explicit pattern store, agents rediscover the same solutions independently, producing divergent naming conventions, inconsistent error handling, and duplicated utilities. Hierarchical memory (working → session → project → global) manages context limits while preserving cross-task learning. The Reflexion paper (Shinn et al., 2023) demonstrates that verbal reinforcement via retained examples measurably improves agent reliability on subsequent similar tasks.

list.item: After a successful task execution where a non-obvious solution was applied — capture before the session ends
When the same problem appears for the second time in a different context — that is a pattern, not a one-off
Before starting a new task wave — inject high-confidence patterns (0.8+) into the task context header
During CLAUDE.md maintenance: sync established patterns (0.9+) into the project rules file
When onboarding a new agent session that lacks project history — load `patterns.md` as working memory
Capturing well-known best practices already in framework docs (e.g., "use async/await")
One-off fixes specific to a single file or edge case
Patterns with confidence below 0.5 (only one use, unverified) — capture as candidate, do not promote
Project-specific configuration values (API keys, URLs) — those go in `.env`, not patterns

rule.statement: A pattern must include the problem context, when to apply, and when NOT to apply — not just the code example.
rule.rationale: Agents conflate "pattern" with "code snippet." A pattern applied in the wrong context is worse than no pattern. The problem context is what enables correct routing.

list.item: Pattern: high abstraction (problem + context + solution), cross-project reusability, metadata (validation, provenance), evolves over time
Code snippet: low abstraction (just code), project-specific, no metadata, static
Code patterns: error handling, data management, API design, testing approaches
Architecture patterns: structural (component org, module boundaries), behavioral (event handling, pub-sub), integration (service communication)
Workflow patterns: planning (estimation, decomposition), execution (implementation order, debugging), review (quality gates, code review practices)

rule.statement: Start every new pattern at confidence 0.5; do not promote to patterns.md until at least 2 uses in distinct contexts.
rule.rationale: Confidence scores are self-assessed by agents and inflate over time without external validation. Requiring 2+ distinct-context uses before promotion prevents premature standardization of single-use fixes.

list.item: Initial (0.5): first successful use — capture as candidate
Validated (0.6-0.7): 2-3 successful uses in similar contexts
Established (0.8-0.9): 5+ uses, verified across different contexts — sync to CLAUDE.md
Proven (0.9+): 10+ uses, adopted as standard practice

rule.statement: Capture a pattern when: the solution works in 2+ different contexts, solves a problem seen before, includes a non-obvious insight, or prevents a common mistake.
rule.rationale: Without explicit extraction at task completion, agents never capture patterns. The extraction must be built into the task completion checklist, not left as an optional afterthought.

list.item: Do capture: solutions to recurring problems, non-obvious approaches, cross-context generalizations, mistake preventers
Do not capture: one-off fixes, well-known best practices (already in docs), project-specific configurations, trivial implementations
Working memory (current task): always loaded, session scope
Session memory (current feature): on-demand, conversation scope
Project memory (.aidocs/memory/patterns.md): persistent, queried by relevance
Global memory: permanent, cross-project — used sparingly

rule.statement: Filter patterns.md by relevance to the current task domain before injecting into task context.
rule.rationale: Loading all patterns into every task context wastes tokens. A payment feature task does not need auth patterns injected unless they share a mechanism.

rule.statement: Calculate confidence using the weighted formula: base_score × usage_factor × success_factor × recency_factor.
rule.rationale: Objective scoring prevents subjective inflation. The recency factor ensures patterns that go unused naturally decay rather than cluttering the active pattern store.

example.code:
confidence = base_score * usage_factor * success_factor * recency_factor
Where: base_score: 0.5 (initial) usage_factor: min(usage_count / 10, 1.5) success_factor: success_rate ^ 0.5 recency_factor: 1.0 - (days_since_last_use / 365) * 0.3

list.item: First successful use: set to 0.5
Successful reuse: +0.05 to +0.10
Failed application: -0.10 to -0.20
No use for 90 days: -0.05
Peer validation: +0.10

rule.statement: Sync patterns with confidence 0.8+ and 5+ uses to CLAUDE.md; never include low-confidence or verbose patterns.
rule.rationale: CLAUDE.md is loaded in every session. Bloating it with unvalidated or verbose patterns wastes context budget and trains agents to ignore the patterns section.

list.item: Include in CLAUDE.md: high-confidence patterns (0.8+), frequently used (5+ uses), project-specific conventions, critical anti-patterns from mistakes.md
Exclude from CLAUDE.md: low-confidence patterns (still validating), obvious patterns (well-known best practices), verbose code examples (link to patterns.md instead)

example.code:
## Learned Patterns
### PAT-001: Async Error Boundary **Confidence:** 0.92 | **Uses:** 15 | **Success:** 93% - Wrap async operations with try-catch-finally - Manage loading/error/data states explicitly - Display appropriate UI for each state See: .aidocs/memory/patterns.md#PAT-001
example.note: Concise summary with link to full pattern. Metrics visible at a glance. No inline code in CLAUDE.md.

rule.statement: Archive patterns unused for 90+ days to patterns-archive.md rather than deleting them.
rule.rationale: Seasonal or infrequent tasks may need patterns that appear dormant. Archiving preserves them while keeping the active store clean.

list.item: Review patterns.md after every 5 completed tasks for duplicates and outdated entries
Merge patterns that solve the same problem in the same context
Add version/context reference to patterns validated against a specific library version
Apply confidence decay formula to patterns unused 90+ days

template.purpose: Full PAT-XXX entry template with problem, solution, confidence, provenance
Minimal quick-capture template for use during execution
Prompts for post-task pattern extraction and periodic review
Shell script syncing high-confidence patterns to CLAUDE.md
